findings.evidence_quote: If you have a simple Form 1040 return only (no forms or schedules except as needed to claim the Earned Income Tax Credit, Child Tax Credit, student loan interest, and Schedule 1-A), you can file for free yourself with TurboTax Free Edition, or you can file with TurboTax Expert Assist Basic at the listed price. Roughly 37% of taxpayers are eligible.
findings.location: boxed callout: 'TurboTax Online: Important Details about Filing Simple Form 1040 Returns'
findings.reasoning: Trigger: TurboTax Free Edition is explicitly mentioned ('you can file for free yourself with TurboTax Free Edition'), so R-01 applies. Requirement analysis: the rule requires that right underneath the free mention, the ~37% qualification stat and the simple-1040-only limitation (with the schedule exceptions) be disclosed. Here, the same sentence/paragraph that mentions the free product also states the simple-1040 limitation (no forms or schedules except EITC, CTC, student loan interest, Schedule 1-A) and immediately follows with 'Roughly 37% of taxpayers are eligible.' Both required elements are present and adjacent, so axis A is satisfied (substantively compliant) even though it's arguably a combined disclosure rather than the mention followed by a separate blurb - the content is functionally equivalent and immediately proximate. Axis B: wording drifts from the approved D-01 text - approved text starts with '~37% of filers qualify. Simple Form 1040 returns only...' whereas this page states the simple-1040 language first, uses 'Roughly 37% of taxpayers are eligible' (not '~37% of filers qualify'), and phrases the schedule exception clause differently ('no forms or schedules except as needed to claim the Earned Income Tax Credit, Child Tax Credit, student loan interest, and Schedule 1-A' vs 'no schedules, except for EITC, CTC, student loan interest, and Schedule 1-A'). This is clear wording drift from the library entry, so axis B is false. Counter-reading considered: one could argue this fails axis A because the order is reversed (limitation stated first, then percentage, not 'right underneath' the free mention in the strict sense the rule describes) - but practically the disclosure text block sits immediately following the free-product mention as a whole unit, satisfying the substantive intent of placement and content. I resolve this as drifted_but_compliant given content is present and proximate, matching the pattern from Worked Example 1.

findings.evidence_quote: TurboTax Free Edition: TurboTax Free Edition ($0 Federal + $0 State + $0 To File) is available for those filing simple Form 1040 returns only (no forms or schedules except as needed to claim the Earned Income Tax Credit, Child Tax Credit, student loan interest, and Schedule 1-A). More details are available here. Roughly 37% of taxpayers qualify.
findings.location: shared footer, 'TurboTax Online/MOBILE OFFERS & PRICING' section
findings.reasoning: Trigger: this footer bullet explicitly names 'TurboTax Free Edition,' triggering R-01 again as a distinct mention. Requirement: immediately following the free-product mention, the simple Form 1040 limitation (with schedule exceptions) and the ~37% qualification stat are both present, satisfying the substantive requirement of the rule (axis A compliant). This matches Worked Example 1 nearly verbatim. Axis B: the approved D-01 text is '~37% of filers qualify. Simple Form 1040 returns only (no schedules, except for EITC, CTC, student loan interest, and Schedule 1-A).' This footer uses 'Roughly 37% of taxpayers qualify' instead of '~37% of filers qualify', and phrases the schedule exceptions in expanded prose rather than the abbreviated 'EITC, CTC' form. This is the same drift pattern explicitly called out as flag/drifted_but_compliant in the worked example, so I follow that precedent exactly. Counter-reading: one might argue this substantially matches (same meaning) and could be treated as 'equivalent' passing axis B, but the worked example explicitly rules that this exact variance constitutes drift, so I follow that precedent.

findings.evidence_quote: TurboTax Free Edition: TurboTax Free Edition ($0 Federal + $0 State + $0 To File) is available for those filing simple Form 1040 returns only (no forms or schedules except as needed to claim the Earned Income Tax Credit, Child Tax Credit, student loan interest, and Schedule 1-A). More details are available here. Roughly 37% of taxpayers qualify.
findings.location: TurboTax Online/Mobile offers & pricing disclosures section
findings.reasoning:
Rule R-01 requires that if TurboTax Free is mentioned, the specific disclosure text "~37% of filers qualify. Simple Form 1040 returns only (no schedules, except for EITC, CTC, student loan interest, and Schedule 1-A)." must appear right underneath. 

On this page, TurboTax Free is clearly mentioned in the "TurboTax Free Edition" bullet within the TurboTax Online/Mobile offers & pricing section. That satisfies the trigger condition.

The nearby disclosure reads: "TurboTax Free Edition ($0 Federal + $0 State + $0 To File) is available for those filing simple Form 1040 returns only (no forms or schedules except as needed to claim the Earned Income Tax Credit, Child Tax Credit, student loan interest, and Schedule 1-A). More details are available here. Roughly 37% of taxpayers qualify." This language contains the substantive elements (simple Form 1040 limitation and a percentage eligibility figure), but R-01 is unusually strict: it mandates that the required wording appear and that it be placed "right underneath." Moreover, the rule ties Axis B to the exact library text D‑01.

Comparing to D‑01: the page uses "Roughly 37% of taxpayers qualify" instead of "~37% of filers qualify" and phrases the simple-return limitation differently ("no forms or schedules" vs "no schedules" and reorders content). Because the rule is canonical and forbids paraphrase, this fails Axis B. Under a strict reading of Axis A, the requirement is also not met, because the rule demands the specific disclosure text, not just equivalent content. The strongest counter-argument is that the current wording is substantively equivalent and directly adjacent, so one might call it compliant-in-substance. However, the instructions for this task and the worked example explicitly treat such paraphrases as drift, not as matching the approved entry, and R‑01 is governed by that template. Thus both axes fail, leading to an unapproved_violation and a flag verdict.

findings.evidence_quote: If you have a simple Form 1040 return only (no forms or schedules except as needed to claim the Earned Income Tax Credit, Child Tax Credit, student loan interest, and Schedule 1-A), you can file for free yourself with TurboTax Free Edition, or you can file with TurboTax Expert Assist Basic at the listed price. Roughly 37% of taxpayers are eligible.
findings.location: TurboTax Online: Important Details about Filing Simple Form 1040 Returns section
findings.reasoning: Trigger met because “file for free … with TurboTax Free Edition” explicitly mentions TurboTax Free. The disclosure includes both elements (simple 1040 limitation and ~37% eligibility) and appears in the same paragraph as the free mention, satisfying Axis A. However, wording drifts from the approved D-01 (“Roughly 37% of taxpayers are eligible” vs “~37% of filers qualify”; and “no forms or schedules” vs “no schedules”), so Axis B fails. Strongest counter-reading: the wording could be treated as equivalent. We reject it because the library requires verbatim-or-equivalent, and prior examples flag similar drift.

findings.evidence_quote: TurboTax Free Edition ($0 Federal + $0 State + $0 To File) is available for those filing simple Form 1040 returns only (no forms or schedules except as needed to claim the Earned Income Tax Credit, Child Tax Credit, student loan interest, and Schedule 1-A). More details are available here. Roughly 37% of taxpayers qualify.
findings.location: TurboTax Online/Mobile Offers & Pricing footnotes — “TurboTax Free Edition” bullet
findings.reasoning: Trigger met via explicit “TurboTax Free Edition … $0 Federal + $0 State + $0 To File.” The bullet includes the simple 1040 limitation and the ~37% eligibility, satisfying Axis A placement because it is within the same bullet as the free claim. Axis B fails due to phrasing drift from D-01 (“Roughly 37% of taxpayers qualify” and “no forms or schedules…”). Strongest counter-reading: consider this verbatim-equivalent. Rejected for the same reasons—materially different wording than the approved library entry.

findings.evidence_quote: TurboTax Free Mobile App Offer: File for free when you start your own taxes in the TurboTax or Credit Karma mobile app by February 28, 2026, 11:59PM ET.
findings.location: TurboTax Online/Mobile Offers & Pricing footnotes — “TurboTax Free Mobile App Offer” bullet
findings.reasoning: Trigger met because it makes a “File for free” claim about TurboTax. The required disclosure (~37% eligibility and simple Form 1040 limitation) does not appear right underneath or within this bullet; the nearest such disclosure is in a separate bullet about Free Edition, which does not satisfy the “right underneath” placement for this specific free mention. Therefore Axis A is not compliant; Axis B also fails. Strongest counter-reading: one could argue this is a separate time-limited promotion not governed by the Free Edition disclosure, or that the adjacent Free Edition bullet suffices. We reject that reading because the rule requires the specific disclosure right underneath each free mention, and this bullet lacks it.
